project_overview.purpose: The Harbor Youth Pathways Initiative is a faith-based, community-anchored program designed to provide safe after-school space, mentoring, academic support, and spiritual encouragement to at-risk middle and high school youth living in the lower-income riverfront neighborhoods of Jacksonville, Florida. The project seeks funding primarily to expand our existing after-school program to an additional neighborhood, increase hours of operation, and add structured vocational exposure workshops that help students envision tangible, hope-filled futures for themselves.
project_overview.project_goals:
The Harbor Youth Pathways Initiative has four primary goals over the next 24 months, each with specific, measurable objectives and clear implementation strategies:

1. PROVIDE SAFE, CONSISTENT AFTER-SCHOOL SPACE
- Objective: Offer a welcoming, supervised, and spiritually nurturing after-school environment to at least 80 unique students per semester (approximately 50 middle school and 30 high school students) across two locations.
- Strategies:
  • Operate the program Monday–Thursday from 3:00–7:00 PM at our existing downtown site and launch a satellite site in the Riverside Terrace neighborhood by March 2026.
  • Maintain a staff-to-student ratio of no more than 1:10, supplemented by vetted volunteers from partner churches.
  • Provide healthy snacks and simple evening meals to any student who needs it, with particular attention to students who report food insecurity at home.
  • Integrate daily devotional time (10–15 minutes) and small-group check-ins focused on emotional and spiritual wellbeing.

2. IMPROVE ACADEMIC PERFORMANCE AND SCHOOL ENGAGEMENT
- Objective: Within one academic year of enrollment, at least 70% of regularly attending students (those who attend 2+ days per week) will show improvement in one or more core subjects (math, reading, or science) as documented by report cards and/or teacher feedback.
- Strategies:
  • Offer structured homework help each day from 3:30–5:00 PM led by staff, retired teachers, and college volunteers from Jacksonville University.
  • Implement bi-weekly literacy circles for middle school students and SAT/ACT prep sessions once per week for high school students.
  • Track student attendance, grades, and teacher feedback (with parental permission) using a simple, secure database; hold quarterly parent meetings to discuss progress.
  • Provide school supplies (notebooks, calculators, flash drives) for students whose families cannot afford them.

3. BUILD POSITIVE MENTORING RELATIONSHIPS AND SPIRITUAL FORMATION
- Objective: Pair at least 50 students with consistent adult mentors and small group leaders who meet with them a minimum of twice per month for one-on-one or small-group mentoring.
- Strategies:
  • Recruit mentors from six partnering churches representing diverse cultural backgrounds, reflecting the demographics of the youth we serve.
  • Require all mentors to complete background checks, a 6-hour training on trauma-informed care, and a 2-hour orientation on our ministry’s faith statement and child protection policies.
  • Develop structured mentoring guides with discussion prompts around topics such as identity, purpose, healthy relationships, conflict resolution, and faith.
  • Host quarterly "Family Nights" where mentors, students, and families share a meal, worship together, and celebrate student achievements (academic, spiritual, and personal).

4. EXPAND VOCATIONAL AND LIFE-SKILLS EXPOSURE
- Objective: Introduce at least 60 high school students to a variety of career pathways and life skills, with at least 40 students completing a 10-session vocational exploration track and 20 students participating in job-shadowing or internship experiences by the end of the project period.
- Strategies:
  • Partner with local businesses, trade schools, and community colleges to host monthly "Career Nights" (e.g., healthcare, trades, public service, technology, ministry).
  • Offer practical life-skills workshops such as financial literacy (budgeting, saving, understanding credit), resume building, interview preparation, and basic time management.
  • Provide bus passes or van transportation to off-site career visits, college tours, and service-learning projects.
  • Encourage students to set at least two concrete post-graduation goals (college, trade school, military, employment, or ministry) and revisit those goals with mentors every semester.

Throughout all goals, we will track outputs (attendance, number of mentoring hours, workshops delivered) and outcomes (grade changes, school attendance, self-reported sense of hope and belonging) through pre- and post-program surveys, school feedback, and parent interviews. Ultimately, our overarching aim is to see youth who might otherwise disengage from school and community instead become Christ-centered, hopeful young adults who are equipped academically, emotionally, and spiritually to pursue their God-given callings.
project_overview.geographic_area_to_be_served: The primary geographic area served will be the urban riverfront and inner-city neighborhoods of Jacksonville, Florida, specifically the Brooklyn, Riverside Terrace, and Mixon Town communities within ZIP codes 32204, 32205, and 32209. These neighborhoods are characterized by high rates of child poverty, single-parent households, and under-resourced public schools. Most of our students attend Matthew W. Gilbert Middle School, Riverside High School, and two nearby charter schools. While our core focus is on youth who live or attend school within a 3–5 mile radius of our main ministry center, we do not turn away students from adjacent neighborhoods as long as we can reasonably provide transportation support or coordinate with their families. The majority of the youth we currently serve are African American and Latino, with a growing number of immigrant and refugee families from Haiti and Central America settling in nearby apartment complexes; these families are also part of our target service area as we expand.

mission__operations_details.where_is_your_current_station_location_i: Our primary station is in Jacksonville, Florida, where we operate in the field on a full-time basis. Our central ministry hub is located near downtown at 742 Evergreen Harbor Drive, Suite 200, within walking distance of several bus lines and three public schools. We are not on sabbatical; our leadership and staff are actively engaged in day-to-day operations, including after-school programming, home visits, and weekend outreach events. In addition to our main office and program space, we partner with Riverside Community Church to use their fellowship hall twice a week for the satellite after-school program, and we regularly bring small teams into nearby apartment complexes and public housing communities for outdoor activities, prayer walks, and family check-ins. Our staff live within or near the neighborhoods we serve, which means our "station" is both our formal office and the surrounding community streets, schools, and homes.
mission__operations_details.describe_the_target_demographics_of_your:
Our mission primarily serves at-risk middle and high school youth ages 11–18 living in lower-income, racially diverse neighborhoods along the riverfront and inner-city areas of Jacksonville, Florida. The majority of the students we work with come from families whose household income is at or below 150% of the federal poverty line. Many live in single-parent households or are being raised by grandparents or extended family members.

Demographically, approximately 55–60% of the youth we currently serve identify as African American, 25–30% as Latino (primarily of Puerto Rican, Mexican, and Central American heritage), 5–10% as Haitian or other Caribbean backgrounds, and the remaining 5–10% as White or multiracial. A growing number of our students are first- or second-generation immigrants. For example, we currently have a small but increasing group of students whose families recently relocated from Honduras and Guatemala, and several students whose parents speak primarily Haitian Creole at home.

Educationally, many of our students attend under-resourced public schools where class sizes are large and access to individualized academic support is limited. A significant portion of our middle school students are reading one to two grade levels behind, and several of our high school students are the first in their families to consider attending college or trade school. Some are navigating Individualized Education Programs (IEPs) for learning differences, and others have experienced school suspensions or chronic absenteeism prior to joining our program.

From a psychosocial standpoint, a number of youth in our program have experienced adverse childhood experiences such as exposure to neighborhood violence, parental incarceration, or housing instability. We also work with students who are coping with anxiety, depression, or grief due to the loss of a family member. While we are a faith-based ministry and openly share the hope of Christ, our doors are open to students of any or no religious background; in practice, our participants include youth from Christian, nominally religious, and non-religious homes, as well as a handful from other faith traditions.

We intentionally seek out students who are: (1) at risk of academic failure or dropping out; (2) lacking consistent adult role models; (3) experiencing food insecurity; or (4) showing early signs of disengagement from healthy community structures (school, church, positive peer groups). Our outreach strategies include school counselor referrals, word-of-mouth among parents, church partnerships, and direct engagement at local parks and apartment complexes. We provide programming in English but have bilingual staff and volunteers who speak Spanish and Haitian Creole to better serve families and help bridge communication gaps with parents and guardians.
mission__operations_details.what_is_your_plan_for_financial_stabilit:
Our plan for financial stability is built on diversification of income streams, careful budgeting, transparent financial practices, and intentional leadership development so that the ministry is not overly dependent on any single person or donor.

1. DIVERSIFIED FUNDING SOURCES
Currently, approximately 40% of our annual budget comes from individual donors (both small monthly givers and a few larger annual donors), 30% from church partnerships, 20% from grants (local foundations and faith-based grantmakers), and 10% from fundraising events. Over the next three years, our goal is to move toward a more balanced mix where no single category exceeds 35–40% of total income.

- Individual Donors: We have about 95 recurring monthly donors who give between $15 and $300 per month. We plan to grow this base to at least 140 recurring donors by the end of 2027 by improving our communication, sharing clear impact stories (including student testimonies and parent feedback), and offering easy online giving options. We send quarterly newsletters, maintain an active email list, and host small "vision dinners" in local homes and churches to invite new supporters into the work.

- Church Partnerships: We currently partner with six local congregations of varying sizes and denominations. These churches provide a mix of financial support, volunteers, and in-kind contributions (facility use, food, and transportation). We are working toward formalizing multi-year partnership agreements with at least three of these churches, ensuring predictable annual support. We also plan to invite two additional congregations into partnership each year by presenting at mission Sundays and regional pastors’ gatherings.

- Grants and Foundations: This grant is part of a broader strategy to secure 4–6 aligned grants per year from foundations that prioritize youth development, education, and faith-based community work. We have created a grant calendar, identified specific prospects, and begun building relationships with program officers. We are also investing in basic staff training for grant writing and reporting so that we can consistently meet requirements and demonstrate impact, which increases the likelihood of renewal.

- Fundraising Events and Earned Income: Each year we host a modest community fundraiser (a 5K run and family festival) that raises between $12,000 and $18,000 and serves as a touchpoint for new donors. We are exploring small-scale earned-income opportunities that align with our mission, such as offering low-cost summer day camps and leadership workshops to partnering churches and organizations (with scholarships for low-income youth), which could generate modest revenue while staying true to our calling.

2. PRUDENT BUDGETING AND RESERVES
We operate with a conservative budgeting philosophy. We build our annual budget on committed or highly likely income rather than optimistic projections. Before expanding any program (such as opening our second site), we aim to secure at least 75% of the first year’s projected costs and a clear pathway for the remaining 25%.

We are also intentionally building a modest operating reserve. At present, we maintain approximately two months of operating expenses in reserve. Our board has adopted a goal of reaching a three- to four-month reserve within the next five years. Any year-end surplus (which has ranged from $4,000 to $12,000 in recent years) is partially allocated to this reserve after addressing any urgent program needs.

3. TRANSPARENCY, ACCOUNTABILITY, AND BOARD OVERSIGHT
We believe that financial stability is deeply connected to trust. We undergo an annual independent financial review conducted by a local CPA familiar with small nonprofits. We share high-level financial summaries with our donors and church partners and make our annual report available on our website.

Our board finance committee meets quarterly to review income and expenses, compare them against the approved budget, and adjust as needed. We use a simple but robust accounting system (QuickBooks Online) with separation of duties in place: one staff member enters expenses, another approves them, and the board treasurer reviews monthly reports. This structure helps prevent mismanagement and reassures donors that funds are being stewarded carefully.

4. LEADERSHIP DEVELOPMENT AND SUCCESSION PLANNING
Because our executive director, Rev. Daniel Ortiz, is a key relational bridge to many donors and churches, we are intentionally spreading responsibility and visibility across the leadership team. Our program director, Sarah Kim, and our development coordinator, Marcus Lee, both regularly speak at churches, write donor updates, and meet with supporters. Over the next three years, we plan to formalize a written succession plan, identify and train potential internal leaders, and document key relationships and processes so the ministry is not overly vulnerable to a single leadership transition.

5. LONG-TERM SUSTAINABILITY FOR THIS SPECIFIC PROJECT
For the Harbor Youth Pathways Initiative, the requested grant funds will primarily underwrite start-up and expansion costs for the second program site (e.g., partial staff salaries, program materials, and transportation). Our long-term plan is for the ongoing operating costs of the satellite site to be covered by a combination of:
- Increased recurring individual donations tied specifically to the new site (we plan a dedicated "Launch Riverside" campaign to recruit 40 new monthly donors).
- A formalized three-year financial commitment from at least one partner church located near the new site.
- Renewal or replacement grants from youth-development funders once we can demonstrate strong outcomes from the first 18–24 months of operation.

In short, we are not viewing this grant as a one-time windfall but as catalytic funding that will allow us to scale responsibly and then sustain that growth through diversified, stable support. By combining careful financial planning, consistent communication with supporters, and strong governance, we believe we can continue serving these youth for many years to come without overextending our resources.
